# Contributing to the Unity Compass

Firstly, thank you for your interest in contributing. This is an unusual project — a philosophical framework built in the open — and contributions look different here than they do on a typical software repository.

## What We're Building

The Unity Compass is developing coordination frameworks for a world that can't agree but can't afford not to. The work spans philosophy, systems thinking, institutional design, game theory, contemplative practice and real-world case studies. If any of that intersects with your experience or expertise, you probably have something to offer.

## How to Contribute

### You don't need to know git.

Seriously. If you have relevant expertise, operational experience or a sharp critique and you've never used GitHub before, the simplest path is:

- **Open an issue**. Click the "Issues" tab and you'll be guided through a set of templates: challenge an argument, offer a case study, share a reference, ask a question, or flag a missing perspective. Each one walks you through what's helpful to include. If none of them fit, there's a freeform option too.
- **Email the author.** sam@aukus.consulting — if you'd rather have a conversation than write a ticket, that works too. The author is open to phone calls and teleconference, and even in-person meetings to those well geographically colocated! Contributions made through conversation will be credited.

### If you are comfortable with git:

- **Issues** are the primary mechanism for discussion, critique and suggestion.
- **Pull requests** are welcome for corrections, additional references, case study contributions and substantive content. PRs will be reviewed by the author.
- **Fork freely.** The licence (CC BY-SA 4.0) means you can adapt this work for your own purposes as long as you credit the source and share improvements.

## What's Most Valuable Right Now

The framework is in early development (v0.1). The most valuable contributions at this stage are:

**Critique.** The [design principles](1-theory/01-core/05-design-principles.md) commit this project to being honest about incompleteness. That commitment is only meaningful if people test it. If an argument is weak, a claim is unsupported, a perspective is missing, or the whole thing is wrong in a way we haven't seen — say so. Constructive challenge is the single most valuable thing you can offer.

**Case studies.** The [case studies directory](1-theory/02-landscape/05-case-studies/) is where this framework meets reality. If you have operational experience of coordination across genuine difference at any scale — from a family dispute to an international negotiation — and you're willing to write it up (or talk through it for someone else to write up), that's gold.

**References and connections.** If you encounter work we should be engaging with — a paper, a book, a practitioner, an existing framework — point us to it. The [current approaches](1-theory/02-landscape/04-current-approaches.md) document is an honest attempt to map the landscape, but no single person can see all of it.

**Perspectives we're missing.** This framework claims to be about coordination across genuine difference. That claim is hollow if the contributors all share the same background. We are actively seeking engagement from people whose expertise, culture, professional context or worldview differs from the author's. Indigenous governance scholars, contemplative practitioners, frontline public servants, people working in non-Western institutional contexts — if the framework doesn't make sense from your perspective, that's important information.

## What's Less Helpful Right Now

- **Copyediting and formatting fixes.** Appreciated but not the priority while the content is still stabilising.
- **Feature requests for tools or platforms.** This is a philosophical framework, not software. The GitHub infrastructure serves the content; the content is the point.
- **Ideological debate for its own sake.** The framework explicitly avoids taking ideological positions. Contributions that argue for a particular political ideology rather than engaging with the coordination problem itself will be respectfully redirected.

## Style and Conventions

If you're contributing written content:

- British English spelling throughout
- No Oxford commas
- Footnotes for citations using GitHub-flavoured markdown (`[^1]:` syntax) with a `---` divider before a References section at the bottom of each document
- Accessible language by default. Academic terminology where it earns its place, plain English everywhere else.
- The full style guide lives in [3-project/meta/](3-project/meta/)

## Recognition

All substantive contributions will be credited. This project is built on the principle that coordination across difference requires incorporating multiple perspectives. That principle applies to how the project itself is developed.

## Code of Conduct

Engage with the same qualities the framework identifies as essential to coordination across difference: genuine perspective-taking, intellectual humility, willingness to hold tension without premature resolution, and respect for the person behind the position you disagree with.

If you can't do that, this isn't the right project for you. If you can, welcome.
